claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to start by asking about your child.

**First, let me ask: How old is the child you're claiming for?**

The age of your child is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'm looking into claiming Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives with me here at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me work through Alex's eligibility.

Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), let me move to the next check in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - since Alex is 8 years old and lives with you, we're on the right track. Now I need to check a few more things:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Given that Alex is only 8 years old, I'm assuming he's not in paid work, but I need to confirm this to continue the assessment. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex is only 8, so no, he's not working at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

What I mean is - is Alex receiving any benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name (not as part of your household claim, but in his own right)?

Again, given that Alex is 8 years old, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular 8-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Next check:

**Is Alex in any kind of apprenticeship program?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, I'm confident he's not in an apprenticeship (these typically start at age 16+), but I need to confirm this to continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - he's way too young for an apprenticeship. He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You would qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** - this is the most common situation
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**So, do you normally live in the UK?** (This is the most straightforward path for most people)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're based in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Since you live in the UK (Manchester), you meet the residency requirement.

Now, the next question is about responsibility for Alex:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You would qualify if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex** - which you've already mentioned you do
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be - this can include food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you've already told me that Alex lives with you at home, this means you meet the responsibility requirement. Is that still correct - Alex lives with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A grandparent
- Another family member

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. If someone else is claiming, they would need to agree to transfer the claim to you, or if there's a dispute, HMRC would need to decide.

So, is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following applies to Alex?**

- Is Alex currently in hospital?
- Is Alex in local authority care?
- Is Alex being fostered?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you?

From what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually... Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months now, but he doesn't come home to stay with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see, thank you for sharing that important information. This does affect the eligibility, so I need to ask some follow-up questions about Alex's care situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Since Alex is in local authority care, there are specific time limits that apply to Child Benefit eligibility.

**Here's the key question: Has Alex been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR does he spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you?**

You mentioned he's been in care for "a couple of months" - can you tell me more specifically:
1. How many weeks has Alex been in care?
2. Does he come home to spend time with you regularly (at least 24 hours per week)?

The rules are:
- Child Benefit can continue for up to **8 weeks** while a child is in care
- OR if the child spends **24 or more hours per week** at home with you, you can continue to claim
- You can also claim again if the child spends 2 consecutive nights per week or 7+ days in a row at home

Could you clarify how long Alex has been in care and whether he comes home for visits?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in care for about 12 weeks now. And no, he doesn't come home to stay with me - he's at the care home full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand, thank you for clarifying that. Unfortunately, this does affect your eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to have to tell you this, but based on your circumstances, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

**Here's why:**

When a child is in local authority care, Child Benefit can only continue for up to **8 weeks**. Since Alex has been in care for 12 weeks and doesn't come home to spend time with you (at least 24 hours per week), you've exceeded this time limit.

**However, there is good news for the future:**

You **can claim Child Benefit again** if Alex's situation changes and he:
- Spends **2 consecutive nights each week** at home with you, OR
- Spends **7 or more days in a row** at home with you

**What you should do:**

1. If you were receiving Child Benefit before Alex went into care, you should inform HMRC that he's been in care for more than 8 weeks so they can stop the payments
2. Keep in touch with the local authority about Alex's care arrangement
3. If Alex starts coming home for regular visits (meeting the criteria above), you can reapply for Child Benefit
4. For advice about your specific situation, contact HMRC directly

**More information:**
- Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-for-children-in-hospital-or-care

I know this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any questions or if Alex's situation changes in the future, please don't hesitate to reach out again.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and lives in local authority care. While Alex meets the basic age requirement (under 16), he has been in local authority care for 12 weeks without spending at least 24 hours per week at home. Under UK Child Benefit rules, Child Benefit stops after a child has been in local authority care for more than 8 consecutive weeks, unless the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home with the claimant. Since Alex has been in care for 12 weeks and does not come home to stay, the 8-week limit has been exceeded, making the claimant ineligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets residency requirements and was responsible for Alex before he entered care. However, Alex has been in local authority care full-time for 12 weeks without spending time at home, which exceeds the 8-week limit for Child Benefit eligibility when a child is in care.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

response.reason: Child has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks in care, unless child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. You can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights each week or 7+ days in a row at home. Contact HMRC about your specific situation.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old and lives in local authority care. While Alex meets the basic age requirement (under 16), he has been in local authority care for 12 weeks without spending at least 24 hours per week at home. Under UK Child Benefit rules, Child Benefit stops after a child has been in local authority care for more than 8 consecutive weeks, unless the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home with the claimant. Since Alex has been in care for 12 weeks and does not come home to stay, the 8-week limit has been exceeded, making the claimant ineligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets residency requirements and was responsible for Alex before he entered care. However, Alex has been in local authority care full-time for 12 weeks without spending time at home, which exceeds the 8-week limit for Child Benefit eligibility when a child is in care.
